title: Using the Best Available Evidence to Personalize Osteoporosis Treatment
authors: E. Michael Lewiecki, MD; Micol S. Rothman, MD

items.title: Why osteoporosis guidelines often fail individual patients
items.text: Guidelines derived from large RCTs are rigorous but restrictive; most clinic patients would not qualify for those trials, limiting direct applicability.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Many or most patients seen in clinical practice would not qualify for participation in the clinical trials supporting the approval of the medications used to treat them"

items.title: How the global osteoporosis treatment gap arose
items.text: Despite excellent fracture-risk tools and approved drugs, most at-risk patients are untreated—now recognized as a global crisis.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "most patients at risk for fractures are not being treated. The large treatment gap is now recognized as a global crisis in the care of patients with osteoporosis."

items.title: How to personalize osteoporosis care beyond guidelines
items.text: Clinical judgment, comorbidities, prior experiences, and patient preferences must supplement guidelines—sometimes requiring multiple visits and varied educational materials.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Clinical judgment, patient preference, and other factors are key aspects of managing complex cases of osteoporosis."

items.title: Why teriparatide is favored over alendronate in high-risk glucocorticoid osteoporosis
items.text: An 18-month head-to-head trial showed teriparatide produced greater spine BMD gain (7.2% vs 3.4%) and lower vertebral fracture rate (0.6% vs 6.1%) than alendronate in GIOP.
items.reference: Case 1: "an 18-month randomized head-to-head trial in 414 patients comparing teriparatide with alendronate in patients with glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis showed larger gains in spine BMD (7.2% vs 3.4%), as well as lower rates of vertebral compression fracture (0.6% vs 6.1%) in the teriparatide-treated group."

items.title: How ASBMR views routine vertebral augmentation
items.text: ASBMR task force advises against routine vertebral augmentation for osteoporotic fractures based on 1-year pain/disability trials—though VAPOUR showed benefit for acute severe pain <6 weeks.
items.reference: Case 1: "A recent American Society for Bone and Mineral Research task force advocated against routine use of vertebral augmentation for vertebral fractures based on a review of multiple trials focusing on 1-year outcomes that showed no differences in pain and disability."

items.title: How to stop denosumab without rebound bone loss
items.text: No drug holiday exists for denosumab—when stopping, start oral alendronate at the next scheduled injection to bridge and prevent rapid bone loss and vertebral fractures.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Start oral alendronate when his next injection is due"

items.title: Why denosumab does not provide lasting protection after stopping
items.text: After stopping denosumab, treatment effect is rapidly lost unless another agent is started—there is no role for a drug holiday.
items.reference: Case 2: "there is no role for a drug holiday with denosumab (thus, Answer A is incorrect). After stopping denosumab, the treatment effect will be rapidly lost if another agent is not started."

items.title: How romosozumab dual-action differs from pure antiresorptives
items.text: Romosozumab inhibits sclerostin, stimulating bone formation via Wnt signaling while reducing resorption through RANKL up-regulation—given monthly ×12 months then followed by antiresorptive therapy.
items.reference: Case 3: "by inhibiting sclerostin, it has a dual action of stimulating bone formation and reducing bone resorption."

items.title: Why romosozumab carries a cardiovascular boxed warning
items.text: ARCH trial showed numerically more cardiovascular events with romosozumab vs alendronate in year 1—FDA boxed warning against use within 12 months of MI or stroke.
items.reference: Case 3: "there is a boxed warning that romosozumab may increase the risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death and should not be initiated in patients who have had a myocardial infarction or stroke within the preceding year (Answer B)."

items.title: ONJ and AFF with romosozumab
items.text: Atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw occurred in FRAME and ARCH trials—likely related to antiresorptive component, not absent.
items.reference: Case 3: "Atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw were reported during both the FRAME and ARCH studies, thought to be related to the antiresorptive action (thus, Answer A is incorrect)."

items.title: Why osteopenia on DXA does not exclude treatment in vertebral fracture
items.text: A low-trauma vertebral fracture establishes osteoporosis diagnosis regardless of T-score—pharmacotherapy is indicated in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Case 1: "This patient has the diagnosis of osteoporosis based on a low-trauma fracture and has a very high risk for future fracture; thus, pharmacologic therapy is indicated (thus, Answer A is incorrect)."

items.title: How Bone Health TeleECHO addresses the treatment gap
items.text: Technology-enabled collaborative learning such as Bone Health TeleECHO is one proposed strategy to reduce the osteoporosis treatment gap.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "many strategies to reduce the treatment gap have been proposed, including technology-enabled collaborative learning, the prototype of which is Bone Health TeleECHO."

items.question: A 62-year-old woman with COPD, chronic oral steroids, and painful vertebral fracture has osteopenia on DXA. Best therapy to reduce future vertebral fractures?
options: Oral alendronate 5 years then drug holiday
items.explanation: Vertebral fracture defines osteoporosis; teriparatide showed superior BMD and vertebral fracture prevention vs alendronate in GIOP trial.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Teriparatide for 18 to 24 months followed by antiresorptive therapy"

items.question: Why is calcium and vitamin D alone insufficient in Case 1?
options: She has osteoporosis by fracture history with very high future fracture risk
items.explanation: Low-trauma vertebral fracture establishes osteoporosis and mandates pharmacotherapy beyond supplements.
items.reference: Case 1: "This patient has the diagnosis of osteoporosis based on a low-trauma fracture and has a very high risk for future fracture; thus, pharmacologic therapy is indicated"

items.question: Regarding kyphoplasty for this patient's vertebral fracture:
options: Routine use reduces adjacent-level fracture risk
It would not be expected to reduce adjacent fracture rate
ASBMR recommends routine augmentation for all vertebral fractures
items.explanation: Vertebral augmentation does not reduce adjacent-level fracture risk; ASBMR advises against routine use.
items.reference: Case 1: "vertebral augmentation would not be expected to reduce the rate of adjacent level fracture."

items.question: When might oral alendronate still be chosen over teriparatide in GIOP?
options: Never — alendronate is contraindicated with steroids
If teriparatide is not financially viable, contraindicated, or not desired
Only if DXA shows osteoporosis not osteopenia
items.explanation: Alendronate remains reasonable when teriparatide is unavailable or declined, though teriparatide has stronger GIOP data.
items.reference: Case 1: "oral alendronate (Answer D) is a reasonable choice if teriparatide is not financially viable, is contraindicated, or is otherwise not desired by the patient"

items.question: A 70-year-old man on denosumab 8 years (eGFR 60, osteopenia on DXA) wants to stop. Best advice?
options: Stop denosumab for a drug holiday
IV zoledronic acid 6 months after last dose
Start oral alendronate when next injection is due
Remain on denosumab for life — no alternatives studied
items.explanation: Bridge with oral alendronate at the scheduled next injection—no denosumab drug holiday exists.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Start oral alendronate"

items.question: Why is single-dose zoledronate at next injection date not the preferred answer in Case 2?
options: Oral alendronate at next injection is the specified best advice in this vignette
items.explanation: The vignette answer is oral alendronate at next scheduled dose; zoledronate bridging is discussed elsewhere but answer C is preferred here.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Start oral alendronate when his next injection is due"

items.question: Which statement about romosozumab is true?
options: No ONJ or AFF occurred in any romosozumab trial
Boxed warning against use within 12 months of MI or stroke
Contraindicated in all patients at osteosarcoma risk
Sclerostin inhibition reduces bone formation via Wnt pathway
items.explanation: FDA boxed warning prohibits initiation within 12 months of MI or stroke; ONJ/AFF did occur; sclerostin inhibits (not stimulates) Wnt signaling.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) There is a boxed warning against using romosozumab in anyone who has had a myocardial infarction in the preceding 12 months"

items.question: Romosozumab's mechanism of action is best described as:
options: Dual anabolic and antiresorptive via sclerostin inhibition
items.explanation: Anti-sclerostin antibody stimulates formation and reduces resorption—a novel dual mechanism.
items.reference: Case 3: "by inhibiting sclerostin, it has a dual action of stimulating bone formation and reducing bone resorption."

items.question: Is romosozumab contraindicated in patients at high osteosarcoma risk?
options: No — no evidence of increased osteosarcoma risk with sclerostin inhibition
items.explanation: No osteosarcoma warning exists for romosozumab unlike teriparatide's rodent data concerns.
items.reference: Case 3: "Since there is no evidence of increased risk of osteosarcoma with sclerostin inhibition in rats or humans, there is no warning against its use in patients previously receiving radiation therapy or otherwise at high risk of osteosarcoma"

items.question: Why may strict RCT-based guidelines not fit many clinic patients?
options: Most treated patients would not have qualified for pivotal trials
DXA is never used in trials
items.explanation: Trial eligibility criteria exclude many real-world patients, limiting guideline applicability.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Many or most patients seen in clinical practice would not qualify for participation in the clinical trials supporting the approval of the medications used to treat them"

items.question: Which factor contributes to the osteoporosis treatment gap?
items.explanation: Fear of adverse effects, poor risk communication, and conflicting guidelines among factors widening the gap.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Many factors contribute to the treatment gap, including fear of medication adverse effects"

items.question: In FRAME, romosozumab for 12 months followed by denosumab showed:
options: 73% reduction in vertebral fracture incidence in year 1 vs placebo
items.explanation: FRAME demonstrated 73% vertebral fracture reduction in first year of romosozumab vs placebo before denosumab continuation.
items.reference: Case 3: "In the first year, the romosozumab group had a 73% reduction in vertebral fracture incidence"

items.question: ARCH trial cardiovascular findings led to:
options: Withdrawal of romosozumab from all markets
Boxed warning and avoidance after recent MI/stroke
items.explanation: Numerically increased CV events in year 1 prompted boxed warning against recent MI/stroke.
items.reference: Case 3: "there is a boxed warning that romosozumab may increase the risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death"

items.statement: Teriparatide reduced vertebral fractures more than alendronate in glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis.
items.explanation: Vertebral fracture rate was 0.6% vs 6.1% favoring teriparatide in the head-to-head trial.
items.reference: Case 1: "lower rates of vertebral compression fracture (0.6% vs 6.1%) in the teriparatide-treated group."

items.statement: A drug holiday is appropriate after 8 years of denosumab if BMD is in the osteopenia range.
items.explanation: There is no role for a denosumab drug holiday—rapid offset causes bone loss without bridging therapy.
items.reference: Case 2: "there is no role for a drug holiday with denosumab"

items.statement: No cases of osteonecrosis of the jaw occurred in romosozumab trials.
items.explanation: ONJ was reported in FRAME and ARCH, attributed to antiresorptive action.
items.reference: Case 3: "osteonecrosis of the jaw were reported during both the FRAME and ARCH studies"

items.statement: Sclerostin acts to increase bone formation via the Wnt pathway.
items.explanation: Sclerostin inhibits Wnt signaling and reduces osteoblastic bone formation.
items.reference: Case 3: "Sclerostin reduces osteoblastic bone formation by binding to LRP5/6, inhibiting Wnt signaling"

items.statement: A low-trauma vertebral fracture establishes an osteoporosis diagnosis regardless of DXA T-score.
items.explanation: Fracture-based diagnosis applies even when DXA shows osteopenia.
items.reference: Case 1: "This patient has the diagnosis of osteoporosis based on a low-trauma fracture"

items.statement: Oral alendronate should be started when the next denosumab injection is due if denosumab is stopped.
items.explanation: Bridging at the next scheduled dose prevents rapid loss of treatment effect.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Start oral alendronate when his next injection is due"

items.statement: Expert-based guidelines are never criticized for subjectivity.
items.explanation: Expert guidelines may be simpler but are criticized for lower evidence levels and subjectivity.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Guidelines developed by experts interpreting the best available medical evidence may be simpler, more intuitive, and easier to apply in clinical practice, but are open to criticism that they are overly subjective"

items.statement: Romosozumab is given monthly for 12 months and should be followed by additional osteoporosis therapy.
items.explanation: Monthly ×12 months then antiresorptive follow-on to maintain/enhance effects.
items.reference: Case 3: "Romosozumab is given subcutaneously by a health care professional once monthly for 12 months and should be followed up with an additional osteoporosis treatment to maintain or enhance its effects."

items.assertion: Assertion: Teriparatide is preferred over alendronate for high-risk glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis with vertebral fracture.
items.reason: Reason: Teriparatide produced greater spine BMD gain and lower vertebral fracture rate than alendronate in a head-to-head trial.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked—trial data support teriparatide for secondary vertebral fracture prevention in GIOP.
items.reference: Case 1: "the strongest data for secondary fracture prevention in this scenario would be for teriparatide (Answer C)."

items.assertion: Assertion: Stopping denosumab without bridging causes rapid loss of treatment effect.
items.reason: Reason: Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody with no lasting skeletal effect after clearance.
items.explanation: Both true—antibody clearance produces rapid rebound bone turnover unlike bisphosphonate persistence.
items.reference: Case 2: "After stopping denosumab, the treatment effect will be rapidly lost if another agent is not started."

items.assertion: Assertion: Romosozumab should not be started within 12 months of myocardial infarction.
items.reason: Reason: ARCH showed numerically more cardiovascular events with romosozumab than alendronate in year 1.
items.explanation: Both true—ARCH data underpin the FDA boxed warning.
items.reference: Case 3: "should not be initiated in patients who have had a myocardial infarction or stroke within the preceding year (Answer B)."

items.assertion: Assertion: Kyphoplasty prevents adjacent vertebral fractures after osteoporotic compression fracture.
items.reason: Reason: Kyphoplasty reduces acute pain in selected patients.
items.explanation: A is false—adjacent fracture prevention is not shown; R true for pain in VAPOUR subset but doesn't explain A.
items.reference: Case 1: "vertebral augmentation would not be expected to reduce the rate of adjacent level fracture."

items.assertion: Assertion: Calcium and vitamin D alone suffice after vertebral fracture if DXA shows osteopenia.
items.reason: Reason: Osteopenia indicates low bone density.
items.explanation: A is false—fracture defines osteoporosis regardless of T-score; R is true but irrelevant to withholding pharmacotherapy.
items.reference: Case 1: "pharmacologic therapy is indicated (thus, Answer A is incorrect)."

items.assertion: Assertion: Romosozumab increases bone formation by inhibiting sclerostin.
items.reason: Reason: Sclerostin normally inhibits Wnt-mediated osteoblastic bone formation.
items.explanation: Both true—inhibiting the inhibitor stimulates formation.
items.reference: Case 3: "Bone formation is increased when sclerostin is inhibited (thus, Answer D is incorrect)."

items.assertion: Assertion: Strict RCT-based guidelines may not apply to many real-world osteoporosis patients.
items.reason: Reason: Most clinic patients would not have met trial inclusion criteria.
items.explanation: Both true—external validity of trials limits one-size-fits-all guideline application.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Many or most patients seen in clinical practice would not qualify for participation in the clinical trials"

items.assertion: Assertion: Oral alendronate at the next scheduled injection is the best bridge when stopping denosumab in Case 2.
items.reason: Reason: Zoledronate has never been studied after denosumab.
items.explanation: A is true per vignette answer; R is false—zoledronate bridging has been studied though alendronate is preferred here.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Start oral alendronate when his next injection is due"
